---
title: "Example notebook"
output: html_notebook
---

A good starting point for any analyses is to create the so-called "R-markdown notebook". Why?

Notebooks are useful to sketch what you want to do, to explore the dataset, to try code and see real-time plots; moreover, it can provide clear and nice reports, with comments and notes that you can share with everybody.

## Code chunks

Code chunks are the actual chunks of the notebook in which you can write your code, execute it and see the results. For example:

```{r}
2 + 2
```

When you save the notebook, you will see both the code and the result in the html report.

## Language

Markdown notebooks are written using the Markdown language, a simple and readable syntax that allows you to format text in different ways when it is rendered.

For example:

* **Titles and subtitles** are created with hashtags:

  * `# Title` → renders as a large heading
  
  * `## Subtitle` → renders as a smaller heading
  
  * `### Subsubtitle` → renders an even smaller heading

* **Quotes** are written using the `>` symbol:
  `> This is a quote`

* **Italic text** is created with single asterisks or underscores:
  `*italic*` or `_italic_`: *example of italic text*

* **Bold text** uses double asterisks or underscores:
  `**bold**` or `__bold__`: **example of bold text**

Markdown is widely used in tools like Jupyter Notebooks, GitHub, and documentation platforms because it is easy to learn and keeps documents clean and readable.

Here are some useful resources to learn more:

* [https://www.markdownguide.org](https://www.markdownguide.org)
* [https://commonmark.org/help/](https://commonmark.org/help/)
* [https://github.com/adam-p/markdown-here/wiki/Markdown-Cheatsheet](https://github.com/adam-p/markdown-here/wiki/Markdown-Cheatsheet)
